items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I have vague misgivings again...
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic mechanism is the mock-heroic, high-brow phrasing «терзают смутные сомнения» applied to petty theft, plus the clipped parallelism «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» meaning both items are now missing (not possessed). The actual translation preserves the elevated “vague misgivings” and correctly renders the loss (“missing”), keeping the parallel structure and the suspicion-callback. Slightly less “mock-heroic” than “haunt/torment,” but still works.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, Tsar—very pleased. Tsar—very pleased, Tsar. Very pleased—hello, Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is the anachronistic, Soviet-bureaucratic politeness loop and repetitive self-introduction as “the Tsar,” made funnier by drunkenness. The translation keeps the repetition and the banal “very pleased,” and keeps “Tsar” as the repeated label. It slightly underplays the “I’m the Tsar” self-identification (it reads more like addressing him as Tsar rather than introducing oneself as Tsar), but the comedic effect largely survives due to the obsessive repetition.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and force; preserves the sudden authoritarian royal persona. No loss of humor or register.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Matches the reference closely and preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy contrasted with the mundane setting. The dash is fine for subtitle rhythm.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved (being walled in / trapped) and “demons” remains the agent. However, “Bricked in!” is a bit slangy/modern and loses some of the melodramatic, archaic complaint tone; also drops the ellipsis-driven rhythm of dawning horror. Still comprehensible and funny in context.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and keeps the religious framing (“life-giving cross”). Slightly less grand/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still conveys the miracle-claim and the joke against a modern mechanical cause.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Functionally works as an imperative to start dancing, but it loses the catchphrase feel and the announcer/MC vibe of the Russian (a set, quotable formula). “Everybody dance!” is understandable yet flatter and less idiomatic/performative than an English equivalent like “Everybody’s dancing!” / “All right—everybody dance!” / “The dance floor’s open!” The comedic ‘command from on high’ is partially preserved, but the iconicity is reduced.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat satisfaction at a fortuitous arrival. It slightly shifts from “I dropped in well” (self-congratulatory timing/choice) to general luck, but the comedic beat (cheerful criminal delight at finding valuables) remains clear and natural in English.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Imported delicacy—eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Successfully avoids calling eggplant spread “caviar,” preserving the key joke (status inversion and Soviet scarcity satire). Keeps the build-up and reveal. “Imported delicacy” is a good functional equivalent for «заморская», though “overseas” would echo the original cadence slightly better. Overall the creative intent is preserved.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,
three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket... three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Preserves the escalating inventory-list comedy and the trailing, self-correcting rhythm with ellipses. “Domestic” is placed naturally (“three domestic cigarette cases”), which slightly reduces the comic awkwardness of the Russian post-position «отечественных» (and the reference’s “cigarette cases, domestic...”), but the overall effect—obsessive counting and status-item boasting—remains. The final “three” is kept, maintaining the punch of the miscount/overcount pattern.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!
And you’ll be cured too!
And I’ll be cured!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved, but the delivery changes: the reference effect relies on calm, almost sing-song resignation with ellipses (a darkly comic acceptance of psychiatric ‘treatment’). The actual version uses exclamation marks and a more emphatic tone, which shifts the humor from deadpan to overt agitation. Still understandable and funny, but it weakens the specific tonal subversion described in the reference analysis.

summary.interpretation: 1.55 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.31 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
